choices.prompt: According to the module, what is the correct order of links in the traceability chain that lets you move in either direction for any change?
choices: work item → branch/commit → pull request → deployment
pull request → work item → deployment → branch/commit
deployment → work item → branch/commit → pull request
branch/commit → work item → pull request → deployment
correct_answers: work item → branch/commit → pull request → deployment

choices.prompt: In the Azure Boards–GitHub integration described, how does a developer create the live, two-way link between a GitHub commit and a Boards work item?
choices: By including the recognized work-item reference syntax (commonly AB#123) in the commit message, PR title, or description
By manually attaching the commit hash inside the Azure Boards work item form after each push
By tagging the deployed commit with the work item number using git tag
By configuring a GitHub Actions workflow that posts the commit to the Boards REST API
correct_answers: By including the recognized work-item reference syntax (commonly AB#123) in the commit message, PR title, or description

choices.prompt: A team finds that its cycle time is short but its lead time is long. Per the module, what does this gap most likely indicate?
choices: Queue and wait time before work actively starts — such as backlog prioritization, waiting for review, or waiting to be picked up
That the active work itself is slow and engineers need more capacity
That deployment frequency is too low and releases should be batched
That time to recovery is rising because incidents are getting harder to fix
correct_answers: Queue and wait time before work actively starts — such as backlog prioritization, waiting for review, or waiting to be picked up

choices.prompt: Which of the following are flow metrics the module names as part of the complete picture of whether a team is fast and stable? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Lead time
Cycle time
Deployment frequency
Number of open pull requests per engineer

choices.prompt: Which practices does the module present as ways to make traceability assemble automatically rather than depending on human diligence? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Reference the work item ID in branch names, commit messages, and the pull request via a linking convention
Tag the deployed commit as a permanent anchor for what shipped
Connect the Azure Boards app to the GitHub repository so AB# mentions auto-link
Require each engineer to manually email a change summary after every deploy
correct_answers: Reference the work item ID in branch names, commit messages, and the pull request via a linking convention
Tag the deployed commit as a permanent anchor for what shipped
Connect the Azure Boards app to the GitHub repository so AB# mentions auto-link

choices.prompt: The module frames the traceability gap with two complementary failures: an on-call engineer who cannot answer "what changed?" and a product manager who cannot tell whether the loyalty-points feature actually shipped. What single underlying problem does the module say these two situations share?
choices: There is no traceable thread connecting a unit of planned work, through the commits and pull request that implemented it, to the deployment that released it
The team is not deploying often enough, so changes accumulate and become hard to attribute
The commit messages are well written but the branch names omit the work item ID
Azure Boards and GitHub are storing duplicate copies of the same work item, causing confusion
correct_answers: There is no traceable thread connecting a unit of planned work, through the commits and pull request that implemented it, to the deployment that released it

choices.prompt: In the traceability chain, the module identifies the "deployment" anchor specifically as something carried over from the previous module. What is that anchor, and why does it serve the role?
choices: The release tag on the deployed commit — it marks exactly what shipped, and from that commit you can find the merged pull requests and then the work items
The pull request description — it lists every commit, so it records what was released
The Azure Boards work item state of "Closed" — it confirms the work was delivered to production
The branch name convention — it carries the work item ID forward into the deployment
correct_answers: The release tag on the deployed commit — it marks exactly what shipped, and from that commit you can find the merged pull requests and then the work items

choices.prompt: Per the module's definitions, which statements about lead time and cycle time are accurate? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Lead time runs from when a request is made (the work item is created) until it is delivered to the customer
Cycle time is the narrower span from when work actively starts ("in progress") until it is done
Lead time minus cycle time is roughly the queue and wait time, often where the real delay hides
Cycle time is the customer's view and lead time is the team's view of how long the work itself takes
correct_answers: Lead time runs from when a request is made (the work item is created) until it is delivered to the customer
Cycle time is the narrower span from when work actively starts ("in progress") until it is done
Lead time minus cycle time is roughly the queue and wait time, often where the real delay hides

choices.prompt: How does the module define "time to recovery" as one of the flow metrics that round out the picture of whether a team is fast and stable?
choices: How long it takes from a production failure until service is restored
How long it takes to roll back a release tag to a previous version
How long a work item waits in the backlog before it is picked up
How long a pull request stays open before it is merged
correct_answers: How long it takes from a production failure until service is restored

choices.prompt: The module lists pitfalls to avoid when building traceability and dashboards. Which of the following does it explicitly call out as pitfalls? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Leaving linking to human diligence instead of baking it into the branch, commit, and PR convention
Tagging nothing, so deployments aren't anchored and "what shipped" is guesswork
Weaponizing flow metrics against individuals, which corrupts the data as everyone games their numbers
Standardizing on the AB#123 syntax before the integration docs confirm it, which permanently breaks all links
correct_answers: Leaving linking to human diligence instead of baking it into the branch, commit, and PR convention
Tagging nothing, so deployments aren't anchored and "what shipped" is guesswork
Weaponizing flow metrics against individuals, which corrupts the data as everyone games their numbers

llm.prompt: Walk through how the integration and conventions in this module let an on-call engineer answer "what changed?" at 2 a.m. after a bad deploy, naming the specific links in the chain and the artifacts that capture them.
llm.reference_answer: The engineer starts from the deployment anchor: the release tag (e.g. v2.5.0) marks exactly which commit shipped. From the failing commit they find the merged pull request, because the PR title and commit message referenced the work item with the recognized syntax (AB#1042) and the Azure Boards–GitHub integration turned that mention into a live two-way link. The work item AB#1042 (the loyalty-points change) then identifies the feature and the why behind the change. This works because each link — work item → branch/commit → pull request → deployment — was captured by the workflow itself (branch/commit conventions, AB# auto-linking, and release tags) rather than left to human diligence. Together the planning link and the deployment anchor let the engineer trace a deployed commit back to its work items in under a minute.

llm.prompt: Compare lead time and cycle time as the module defines them, and explain why the gap between them is the most useful thing to watch on a dashboard.
llm.reference_answer: Lead time runs from when a request is made (the work item is created) until it is delivered to the customer — the customer's view of how long until they get what they asked for. Cycle time is the narrower span from when work actively starts ("in progress") until it is done — the team's view of how long the work itself takes. Lead time minus cycle time is roughly the queue and wait time, which is often where the real delay hides. Watching the gap is valuable because a short cycle time with a long lead time means the work is fast but waits before starting — in backlog prioritization, review, or to be picked up — pointing to a process problem to fix rather than a person to push. Reporting one metric as the other hides exactly the queue time you most need to see.

llm.prompt: Explain why the module insists that flow metrics measure the system and not individuals, and describe what goes wrong if a manager uses cycle time to rank engineers.
llm.reference_answer: Flow metrics like lead time, cycle time, deployment frequency, and time to recovery describe how work flows through the system, which the module calls the only honest way to use them. Their value is diagnostic — exposing where flow stalls, such as a widening lead-minus-cycle gap that signals queuing, or a rising recovery time that signals incidents are getting harder to fix — so the team can act on causes. If a manager uses cycle time to rank individuals, people game their numbers by splitting tickets or avoiding hard work, which corrupts the data and the team. That destroys the metric's diagnostic value: the rule is measure the flow and fix the system, not weaponize the numbers against people.
